items.title: Social Security and SSI payments rise 2.8% in 2026 (new SSA fact sheet details)
items.summary: SSA’s 2026 COLA is 2.8%, effective with benefits payable in January 2026. The SSA fact sheet also lists updated 2026 thresholds including the $184,500 maximum taxable earnings cap and SSI federal payment standards of $994/month for an individual and $1,491/month for a couple.

items.title: IRS: 2026 filing season opened Jan. 26; most EITC/ACTC refunds expected by March 2
items.summary: The IRS says the 2026 filing season opened January 26, 2026 (for tax year 2025 returns). For many early filers claiming the Earned Income Tax Credit (EITC) or Additional Child Tax Credit (ACTC), the IRS expects refunds to be available by March 2, 2026 if they choose direct deposit and there are no other issues with the return.

items.title: Federal Student Aid: 2025–26 FAFSA must be received by June 30, 2026
items.summary: The official 2025–26 FAFSA form states that for federal aid, the Department of Education must receive the application no later than June 30, 2026 (with submission beginning as early as Oct. 1, 2024). The form also notes schools must have correct, complete information by the student’s last day of enrollment in the 2025–26 school year.
items.source: Federal Student Aid (U.S. Department of Education)

items.title: CMS issues guidance for new limits on federal Medicaid/CHIP matching funds for certain noncitizens (effective Oct. 1, 2026)
items.summary: CMS announced guidance to implement a statutory change that limits states’ ability to claim federal Medicaid and CHIP matching funds for individuals who are not U.S. citizens/nationals or who fall into specified noncitizen categories. CMS says the change begins October 1, 2026, with exceptions including continued matching funds for treatment of emergency medical conditions and no change to the option for states to cover lawfully residing children and pregnant women.

items.title: USDA sets FY 2026 WIC cash-value benefit amounts; states must allow at least one non-fresh produce form by April 2026
items.summary: USDA’s WIC policy memo sets FY 2026 monthly fruit-and-vegetable cash-value benefit amounts at $26 for children, $48 for pregnant/postpartum participants, and $52 for fully or mostly breastfeeding participants. The memo also reiterates that state agencies must authorize at least one form of fruits and vegetables in addition to fresh (frozen, canned, and/or dried) by April 2026.

items.title: USDA reminder: SNAP general work rules include 80-hour monthly requirement for many adults, with exemptions
items.summary: USDA’s SNAP work requirements page explains that some adults must work or participate in a work program for at least 80 hours per month (or a combination) to meet program rules, with exemptions such as caregiving for a child under 6, disability limitations, or participation in treatment programs. The page also outlines separate time-limit-related rules and exemption categories for ABAWD requirements.

items.title: USDA publishes FY 2026 SNAP cost-of-living adjustment (COLA) tables
items.summary: USDA has published FY 2026 SNAP cost-of-living adjustment (COLA) allotment tables for the 48 contiguous states and D.C. and for other jurisdictions. Households can use the tables to see updated maximum allotments, income eligibility standards, and deduction amounts used in benefit calculations.

items.title: Federal Student Aid: 2025–26 Pell Grant maximum is $7,395; minimum is $740 (effective July 1, 2025–June 30, 2026)
items.summary: A Federal Student Aid Dear Colleague Letter notes that under continuing appropriations, the 2025–26 award-year maximum Federal Pell Grant remains fixed at $7,395 and the minimum award is 10% of the maximum (rounded), or $740. The update states these amounts are effective July 1, 2025 through June 30, 2026.
items.source: Federal Student Aid (U.S. Department of Education)
